summary: O'Neil examines the ongoing rebellion of law schools against US News & World Report rankings, sparked partly by revelations that Columbia falsified self-reported data. She traces how the rankings became the first 'Weapon of Math Destruction'—an opaque scoring system that warped higher education by incentivizing gaming rather than genuine improvement. The current negotiation between US News and law deans over formula tweaks perfectly illustrates her core thesis: algorithms are opinions embedded in math. The disagreement over what factors to weight reveals that rankings have always been subjective value judgments masquerading as objective measurement.
key_points: Columbia was caught lying about self-reported data, triggering widespread scrutiny of the rankings system
The rankings caused a destructive feedback loop: colleges gamed metrics, education worsened, costs rose, and administrative bloat increased
US News and law deans want completely different formula adjustments, proving the rankings are subjective value choices
The rankings' power comes from public trust in numbers that 'seem scientific' rather than actual validity
US News's proposed tweaks ignore student debt and school spending while deans want credit for diversity and financial aid
money_quotes: It was essentially the first Weapon of Math Destruction, if you will, because it was the first opaque scoring system that had widespread and devastating impact.

They ended up gaming it so badly that everything got warped, including most importantly the lives of teenagers, and education got worse, and most expensive, and nobody won, except maybe the huge number of people who now work in administration.
Algorithms are opinions embedded in math.
These are all stabs in the dark as to how a given person might actually decide to value a given school.
It's all opinion, it's always been opinion.
Their opinions are archaic, undependable, and totally reliant on a public that just trusts numbers because they 'seem scientific.'
key_insight: The negotiation over ranking formula changes exposes that algorithmic scores are not objective truths but subjective value judgments that derive false authority from mathematical presentation.
